title: Pisto manchego
subtitle: Spanish braised summer vegetables
excerpt: Aubergine, courgette, and peppers cooked down slowly in olive oil and tomato until soft, silky, and deeply flavoured. The Spanish cousin of ratatouille, simpler in approach, harder to stop eating. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Pisto manchego is the classic braised vegetable dish of La Mancha in central Spain, documented by Claudia Roden in The Food of Spain (2011) and Penelope Casas in The Foods and Wines of Spain (1982). Regional variants exist across Spain under various names; the Manchegan version typically includes aubergine alongside the peppers and courgette.

content.text: Pisto requires patience and olive oil. The vegetables need to cook long enough to completely surrender their texture. They should be soft and almost jammy by the end, not tender-crisp. The olive oil is not a cooking medium to be minimised but part of the dish; it absorbs the flavour of the vegetables and carries it back into each spoonful.

The traditional Manchegan version is served with a fried egg on top, which turns it from a side dish into a meal. It is also good with bread, alongside grilled meat or fish, or as a filling for omelettes. Cold from the fridge the next day, straight from the container, it is also one of the better things you can eat standing at the kitchen counter.

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: use good oil. It becomes part of the sauce

content.text: A heavy-based casserole or Dutch oven gives the best result, the thick base prevents scorching during the long cook. A wide frying pan works for smaller quantities.

Warm {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a heavy casserole over medium heat. Add {{onion}} finely chopped onion and cook for 10 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika; stir for 1 minute. Add the aubergine and peppers, season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and stir to coat. Cook for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally. Add the courgette and stir everything together. Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes. Reduce the heat to low, cover with a lid slightly ajar, and cook for 25 to 30 minutes until all the vegetables are very soft and the oil has separated slightly to the surface. Taste for seasoning. Serve warm or at room temperature.

items.fix: Increase heat slightly and add a splash of water. Cover more tightly for the last 10 minutes to trap steam.
items.cause: Heat too low, or pan too wide so the vegetables dried out rather than braised.

items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 10 minutes and cook over medium heat until the excess liquid evaporates. Pisto should be thick and oily, not soupy.
items.cause: Lid on too tightly, or courgette added too early before other vegetables had reduced.

items.fix: Aubergine takes longer than the other vegetables. Add it first and give it 10 full minutes before the courgette goes in.
items.cause: Not cooked long enough, aubergine needs sustained heat to lose its texture.

content.text: Pisto comes from La Mancha in central Spain, the flat dry plateau of Cervantes and Don Quixote. Summer vegetables grow well there, and the cooking is straightforward and generous with olive oil. The name probably comes from the Aragonese word for a mixture cooked together. Pisto is close kin to French ratatouille and Italian caponata. All three slow-cook summer vegetables in olive oil until they are fully transformed. In La Mancha, pisto with a fried egg is the classic weekday lunch. The egg breaks into the warm vegetables at the table.
